zotero.abstractNote: Scaling laws describe the predictable decrease in test loss with increasing compute, parameters, or data. Recent research has shown that the loss on one dataset (e.g., SlimPajama) often follows a linear relationship with the loss on another dataset (e.g., C4) when compute is varied. This phenomenon, which we refer to as loss-to-loss scaling, is observed across models trained on the same data distribution but differs for models trained on different data distributions. In this work, we investigate the factors that determine loss-to-loss scaling laws. We first show that these scaling laws are consistent across a wide range of model architectures (e.g., Transformers, RWKVs, State-Space Models) and model sizes if they are trained on the same data. In contrast, the data distribution has a significant impact: even subtle changes in data composition lead to distinct lines. Through a series of controlled experiments, we show that the slope and intercept of the loss-to-loss scaling law are determined by the relative data quality and the data overlap between the training and test distributions. Our findings suggest that loss-to-loss scaling laws can serve as a powerful tool to characterize and compare data distributions, offering a lens through which to understand the complex interplay between data and model performance.

notes.note: Publication Title: arXiv (Cornell University) Publisher: MPI-IS and University of Tübingen
